posts.author: Belal Sucks
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Dumont vs. Santos
Norma Dumont, DEC
UFC might have to rig another Dumont win on the scorecards again here but it is what it is that juicy booty is getting a title shot after this whether we like it or not.

Vieira vs. McConico
Rodolfo Vieira, DEC
Jesus Christ dude if this remains a striking match then were just gonna watch these two bums stare at eachother for 15 minutes. Gut feeling tells me Viera wins just because McConnico should be 0-3 in the UFC right now.

Filho vs. Rocha
Jafel Filho, SUB, R2
I remember Clayton Carpenter making this guy look horrible. Easy sub for Filho here.

Dumas vs. McVey
Jackson McVey, SUB, R1
OMG the amount of matchups on here between literal jobbers is crazy. I think McVey uses his size and BJJ to snatch up a submission here early. Dumas has ridiculously bad choke defense and struggled with previous opponents of a similar skill set.

Grant vs. Luna Martinetti
Adrian Luna Martinetti, DEC
Wow an actual banger matchup on this card looks so out of place. My heart wants to pick Davey here but I actually think this new guy is going to be pretty good. Showed a lot of heart and the ability to put huge striking numbers up over the course of 3 rounds on his DWCS win. Davey being 40 and coming off a bad KO loss makes me worry a bit too much in this matchup against a young and hungry opponent entering his prime.

posts.author: Belal Sucks
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Brady vs. Buckley
Sean Brady, DEC
Man... This is one of those fights that I cant feel too confident picking either side based on the matchup and how they looked in their last performance. We're gonna find out real early if Brady's glass jaw or Buckley's shoddy defense is bigger Achilles heel. I could definitely see a world where Buckley steps on the gas right away, pressures him, puts his back against the cage and finds the TKO but I think the smarter pick here is Brady by decision. We've seen him raggdoll and be dominant against stronger grapplers than Buckley and I just dont trust Buckley's fight IQ at times. If Brady gets chinned horrifically again though I could see a potential Gilbert Burns or Luque level of fall off in the near future.

posts.author: B7L7
posts.content:
[Predictions]

If this was a 3 round fight it would really favor Aljo, but I think at this point in his career he can't sustain high pace across 5 rounds, and he seems less durable than ever. He got tired in a 3 round fight against Movsar which is normal since it was full of super high paced chain-wrestling sequences, but to me it's super clear why he fought with low pace and somewhat low output in a dominant win against Ortega: he simply doesn't have 5 round cardio.  I don't think Zalal gets tired easily and is a good striker at range. Good luck to Aljo trying to catch him across the octagon for 5 rounds without completely gassing out. If he can get the takedonws in early and win rounds 1-3 by establishing significant ground control time, then Zalal should be tired aswell, but I don't think it will happen.

posts.author: battousai
posts.content:
[Predictions]

@Bilinho
You're over analyzing the fact that Aljo had to fly to the other side of the planet for a fight where he didn't even complete his weight cut. I don't think Aljo was probably super locked in for that one. There was a point where he probably thought he flew halfway around the world for nothing and doubted whether the fight would even happen.

Aljo took a round off Evloev and I don't remember him looking anymore tired than Evloev afterwards. That fight was just super competitive. No offense, but it feels like you're trying to manufacture a narrative here that's not supported by much evidence. I think there's honestly a better argument to be made we know 0 about Zalal's sustained cardio in a long, drawn out 5 round fight. Zalal has only seen a 3rd round once in the last +3 years and is 4-5 in his career in fights that go the distance.

I could easily see Aljo taking 3 rounds from him with how good of a grappler he is. That is what Zalal has been coasting off of his most recent run but he's not just gonna coast against Aljo. Zalal has also just faced 0 adversity in a really long time and I could see him struggling here against someone who's got multiple main event wins banked at this level. Just my personal opinion, but it's hard not to lean on Aljo's elite experience here.

I lean savvy vet performance by Aljo here, 3-2 or 4-1 on the scorecards.

posts.author: Belal Sucks
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Sterling vs. Zalal
Aljamain Sterling, DEC
Oh god this going to be hard to watch for 5 rounds but I think Aljo is the much superior fighter here in pretty much every aspect. Aljo is insanely strong and his grappling dominance hasnt skipped a beat since moving up to 145lbs. I clown on him a lot for being boring but honestly this guy is one of the most dominant fighters of this era and one of the best MMA grapplers of all time. Zalal is good and very well rounded no doubt but he's got pillow hands which plays a part in this because the guys who have beaten Aljo are typically knocking him out or hurting him badly on the feet. I think Aljo mop up the octagon with Zala via wrestling dominance here but I tell you what; if Zalal wins this one Im predicting right now that he's a future champ. The Belal comparisons were a funny joke at first but the parallels between the two are becoming scary.

Jackson vs. Barcelos
Montel Jackson, TKO, R2
IDK what on earth Jackson was doing last fight but he's such a tough matchup for Raoni that I kind of lean towards him. Barcelos is old and kind of chinny and he's won his most recent fights using his grappling but idk if he's gonna have that same success against Jackson in this one. If Jackson keeps this one on the feet he should knock out Barcelos.

Dumont vs. Edwards

posts.author: JeanSilvaEnjoyer
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Sterling vs. Zalal
Youssef Zalal, DEC
I feel strongly that Aljo is on the decline. Those first four rounds against Ortega were hugely uneventful—basically just pitter-patter striking where Aljo was clearly getting the better of the exchanges but by a pretty thin margin. Then, in R5, Ortega flipped a switch because his gameplan was clearly just to tire Aljo out and see if he could find a sub or a TKO late in the fight. Ortega stung Aljo with a nice overhand, and after that, Herb Dean had to get in the middle to tell Aljo that he needed to stop running away. It looked like Ortega might have been on the verge of a finish, but Aljo got a takedown and spent the remainder of the fight in top position. I thought it should have been 49-46, but I think Aljo got 50-45 from all three judges. Regardless, it must be mentioned this was a version of Brian Ortega who nearly collapsed during weigh-ins and who had been hospitalized only days before the fight took place in Shanghai. Zalal is 7 years younger than Aljo, he's got the wrestling, he's got the striking, he's got the cardio. I think he is good enough to beat this declining version of the former champ.

Dumont vs. Edwards
Norma Dumont, DEC
Uhhh I don't know. Edwards has impressed me quite a bit with her improvement over the past few fights, but her level of competition has not been the best. Her best win is Cornolle in the rematch, and I still don't think Cornolle is all that good. I mean, look what Karol Rosa did to her in the fight before the Edwards rematch. So, I'm going to pick Dumont, but I'm not loving it.

García vs. Hernandez
Alexander Hernandez, TKO, R3
That performance against Jared Gordon was pretty impressive, but apparently Gordon had been hit by a car the day before the fight or something like that, so I'm not sure how much stock to put in Rafa's win there. Plus, Alex Hernandez has been looking better since committing to the move up to 155, with that CDF knockout being being particularly impressive. I was going to pick Alex to beat MJ before the suspicious line movement led to the fight being canceled, and I think in general that Alex has better accomplishments on his resume than beating washed Vinc Pichel, washed Clay Guida, and car crash Jared Gordon.

Grant vs. Luna Martinetti
Davey Grant, DEC
One last ride, Davey? I'm worried about how Davey is going to look coming off of a brutal knockout, plus the fact that he's facing a 17 and 1 prospect. But Luna looked so hittable on that DWCS fight, and Grant is so quick for his age. He's not going to stand around waiting to get hit like Vologdin did. And unless Davey's deteriorated a lot since the Blackshear fight, he's still got cardio and heart for days. Plus, the dude can still crack. He was on the verge of TKO'ing Blackshear in R3 of their bout. I picked Grant to beat Jourdain and we all saw how that went, so maybe I'm making the same mistake again here. Might switch, but Charles Jourdain's speed and movement is, as far as I can tell, leaps and bounds more difficult to deal with than what I've seen from Martinetti.

Jackson vs. Barcelos
Montel Jackson, DEC
I'm not loving this pick, either. Montel looked woeful in that fight with Figgy. He seemed like he was terrified of getting taken down and just couldn't pull the trigger. You would think that this loss would be enough to show him the error of his ways, but Figgy only shot like five takedowns and only got two of them. If Barcelos wants, he can shoot all day and never stop. I also really don't like Jackson's camp, which seems to have no guys who can match the caliber of training partner he needs. Still, I think that the late career run of Raoni Barcelos has to come to an end somewhere, and his wins have been just a tad sus. Talbott didn't know how to wrestle, Cody Garbrandt is washed, Christian Quinonez hurt Barcelos early and is also kind of garbage, and Ricky Simon seems like he might be fading badly based on how he looked in the subsequent Yanez fight. So, I lean Jackson either by an early KO or by a narrow decision.

Buchecha vs. Spann
Ryan Spann, TKO, R1
Somehow, Buchecha is the favorite despite 77% of Tapology picking Spann. I don't know what exactly is going on, and I also know that I hate to ever trust Ryan Spann, but come on. This Marcus Buchecha guy has just looked absolutely awful. Before fighting Buchecha, Kennedy Nzechukwu got taken down in 45 seconds by the goofiest looking shot of all time from Valter Walker, then heel hooked in an instant. Meanwhile, BJJ prodify Marcus Buchecha takes Kennedy down three times and earns a draw solely because of an illegal eye poke. I hate to pick short notice Superman Ryan Spann, but he's got a lot of power, and his losses are all to better guys than Buchecha.

Vieira vs. McConico
Rodolfo Vieira, SUB, R2
I'm gonna be honest, I really want to pull the trigger and pick McConico. Dolfo has seemed like he's degrading quickly. Five months ago, he got bodybagged by Robert Nickal, and not too long before that, he lost to Andre Petroski of all people as a massive favorite. And Petroski has since looked like a shell of himself in the two subsequent fights since he beat Vieira—out-pointed by Edmen Shabazyan (who gets outpointed by Edmen Shahbazyan?) and KO'd with a glancing shot by goofy ahh Cam Rowston. Vieira's takedowns are pretty terrible. He's got 21% accuracy, and he shoots like a dozen takedowns every fight while only being able to get one or two. Eric McConico is nothing to write home about, but I mean, I think Susurkaev and Ruziboev would do the exact same thing to Rodolfo that they did to Eric. Eric's never been subbed in 15 pro fights, but his cardio looked pretty bad in the Brundage fight, and he kept looking to his corner with desperation in his eyes when he was on the mat as if he didn't know how to grapple. He was able to get back to his feet pretty quickly whenever he was taken down, and Brundage was taking deep, committed shots to get Eric down in the first place. Ah, whatever. I'll go Dolf. At least he has real wins in the UFC. But I might switch.

Dumas vs. McVey
Jackson McVey, TKO, R1
Listen, this is a BADASS fight, kid. Two of the all time greats in the middleweight division. This is actually kind of a winnable fight for Dumas. McVey's grappling is nonexistent. He tapped almost as soon as he went to the mat with Brunno Ferreira, and he was piecing up Zach Reese badly until Reese got him to the mat and found the rear naked choke. So, I have very little confidence in McVey. But I just can't pick Sedriques "Boonk Gang" Dumas to win a fight in 2026. All he does is smoke weed and commit crime. And make YouTube videos. At least McVey is likely taking his career seriously.

Bueno Silva vs. Montague
Michelle Montague, DEC
MBS is washed

Griffin vs. Valenzuela
Victor Valenzuela, TKO, R1
I mean, this is a DWCS loser against a vet who just went to a split decision with Chris "Action Man" Curtis, AND took a round off of Michael Morales, AND went to a DEC with Morales. The more I think about this, the less confident I am in Valenzuela, but he's 8 years younger against a 40 year old man who was never all that good to begin with.

Alencar vs. Polastri
Julia Polastri, DEC
Close fight here. Polastri plays around too much in the cage, and I can definitely see a world where Alencar gets her on the ground and wins via control time in the same way that Jasmine and Loopy did, but I think Jasmine and Loopy are better than Alencar, and I think Polastri is going to keep improving given how young she is to be at this level in the UFC. On the feet, I don't think it will be close, and Alencar seemed to be fading pretty badly by R3 of her fight with Carnelossi even though she was having the exact kind of fight she wanted. Plus, it is hard not to have any questions about those 9 grappling losses on Alencar's resume in the first half of her career. Obviously, the fact that she's competing and losing means that she's getting better, but it still makes me hesitant to pick her over a dangerous up and comer.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Viera vs McConico
McConico DEC

I have no idea why this is the featured prelim, this is going to be a horrible fight; we’ve already seen this exact same fight twice before in Veira vs Gore/Petroski, and both were horrible fights. I’ve always been partial to Viera but there has been a lot of regression in his game the last few years. I was always impressed with his game for someone who was a pure BJJ guy but as he’s gotten older, Viera has slowed down a ton. His wrestling was always limited but he got by because he has an explosive blast double and was a good athlete. However, he’s 36 going on 37 and has had a slew of health issues, so that has declined. He’s still strong and in very good shape but he has lost a lot of his explosivity; when he shoots now, it’s telegraphed and slow. He pretty much only has a blast double as well, which makes it really difficult for him to effectively ground his opponents. His bjj is elite but without the wrestling to get it there, it’s pretty much irrelevant. I’ve always thought Viera was a pretty serviceable striker for a BJJ guy, but that too has declined. He was never going to win exclusively with his jab but he did a very good job of using it in the past to deter opponents. However now that too has become predictable. Viera doesn’t have enough power or a long enough reach to command respect, and since all he throws is a jab, it’s easy to get ahead on volume. This is a very strange fight because on paper it should be a layup for Viera, but I’ve got a weird feeling about this one. McConico isn’t bad but he’s an NPC who is only here because of short notice; he is an MMA Lab mainstay who just kind of goes through the motions. There’s nothing special about his game but he’s also a very solid guy; he has a long reach, decent technical skills, solid tdd, good athleticism, good cardio, etc. The main issue with him is that he’s extremely chinny, but that won’t be a factor here since Viera is as well. I was originally thinking this was an easy sub to get Viera back in the win column, but the Gore/Petroiski fights are really giving me pause. Those two are both really bad fighters but they have good enough tdd (like McConico, albeit worse than both) and gave Viera fits. His attempts were sluggish and poor, and the one time he did get both down, they popped back up pretty easily. McConico can be taken and held down but his first layer defense is pretty good and he pops up pretty easily. The Brundage fight was a really bad look (especially since he arguably lost), but it is worth noting that was up a weight class on one week’s notice. As terrible as Brundage is, he is also much more explosive and a better athlete/wrestler than Viera is. He did score tds with some control but his entires were also much better than Viera’s there. It’s a really bad look seeing McConico stay there for a little, but Brundage also couldn’t do anything but hug him there. I expect Viera to get a takedown or two but I honestly don’t see him having a ton of success with his usual control gameplan. In that instance, this immediately goes from a -250 fight to a pickem imo. Without his grappling there is nothing special about Viera at all, and I honestly would put him near the bottom of the division in terms of pure striking. He lost a decision (albeit close) to Petroski of all people on the feet, and the only reason he beat Gore was because he was too hesitant to throw. The latter is a bit of a worry for me since McConico has really bad output issues (and it’s why he almost lost to Brundage), but at the same time McConico has a big reach advantage and is definitely the better striker here. His 77 inch reach is gonna make it really hard for Viera to get his jab off, and I think that’s a huge issue since his striking revolves entirely around that. McConico is also simply put a better striker than Viera; in a striking match at range I think he is much sharper and significantly more comfortable on the feet. There are also some intangibles to note here as well. Firstly, Viera is coming off of an absolutely vicious knockout. I don’t expect him to be chinny and Bo is obviously levels above McConico, but at his age I expect some regression from a loss like that. We already saw a clear decline these last three years and that may be the straw that broke the camel’s back. Another interesting angle is that McConico trained and cornered Gore for the Viera fight, so he knows the gameplan and what to expect here. I expect this to be a really boring fight but I think McConico has the ability to counter the wrestling here. In that scenario, this will be a competitive and boring striking match at range. However, in that type of fight I think McConico is the better martial artist and much more comfortable striking at range.

Dumas vs McVey
McVey SUB RD 1

Typical loser-goes-home slop but this should be a hilarious fight. Dumas fights are usually very tough to watch but the McVey fights, I think this should be a guaranteed finish. These guys are about as bad as it gets for men’s MMA but imo there are levels to the **** here. McVey is not UFC level but he at least has some skills, he just got in the UFC way too soon. Dumas on the other hand is a terrible fighter who has overstayed his welcome because the matchmakers weirdly love him. None of his losses have been competitive fights and with the exception of Michal he’s not even losing to great fighters. His wins are against 3 of the worst guys to ever step foot in the ufc and Dumas barely sc****d by all of them. Brundage fight was hardly a real win as he showed up to lose, I think Dumas lost to Abu, and he gassed out and barely sc****d by .500 brawler Denis Tiuliulin. Pre tape I was thinking Dumas could survive the onslaught and get a comeback finish, but he is harmless and literally does nothing outside of r1. He gasses too, so I don’t think he has some massive cardio edge over McVey. Seeing McVey quit the first time he left r1 was a really bad look but tbf, that pace he set in r1 was wild; I actually don’t think Dumas could survive an onslaught like that. Even if he does, I don’t think his cardio/heart is much better than McVey’s; the latter is actually awful imo. Dumas quits if you give him the door and isn’t even durable either. He reacts terribly to being hit (rocked against both Johnson and Denis) and his KO losses aren’t even real KO’s imo; he got hit and just curled up into a ball. He has no grappling at all either, his two sub losses are to cans and they were meme subs. The Johnson loss is just wild; getting subbed by him is bad enough but it was more of a head squeeze than a real guillotine. He literally just grabbed his windpipe and squeezed from side control; Dumas was literally positioned to tap before the choke was even in. This goes back to my point about levels, both guys suck but McVey has at least moved to a real gym and has some decent skills (Size, aggression, decent chokes), Dumas is fighting for paychecks only and is a complete mental case outside of fighting. McVey still doesn’t seem to be comfortable striking at range, but I think he can push this ot the clinch very easily. Dumas doesn’t fight behind his reach or really even know how to strike, he just flails his arms around wildly. This will open opportunities for McVey to get to the clinch, and I think he can just bully his way into it as it is since Dumas doesn’t have good footwork or grappling. In the clinch McVey is still bad, but he ahs some actual skills there. The knees and elbows he was throwing against Reese were actually really solid, and he came very close to knocking him out there as well; it’s just semantics but I don’t think Dumas is toughing out those knees if he gets hit there. That type of aggression is not something Dumas likes at all, and if you watch enough tape he panics every time he gets hit. McVey doesn’t seem very powerful to me but you don’t need much power to hurt Dumas. McVey’s wrestling and honestly grappling is pretty fraudulent, but I think he should be okay here. Dumas will try to wrestle in almost every fight but he is one of the worst ever. Sadly enough I think he can take McVey down but as bad as he is off of hius back, I think his grappling is good enough to be fine. Dumas does nothing with his top control and doesn’t even have a white belt; McVey hitting that reversal at the end of r1 and using half guard to shrimp out tells me that he is capable enough of a grappler. He should be able to get to his feet or reverse position, and if he gets on top I think Dumas is cooked. He has no game off of his back and like I’ve said, his bjj is just nonexistent. I also think a McVey meme sub from the clinch is very live; almost all of his LFA fights he gets these jobbers to panic and then they put their necks right into a sub. Dumas is a bad enough of a fighter/grappler that I could 100% see him curling over from a clinch knee and putting his neck right into a D’arce; I hate to be so repetitive but this guy literally quit to a light squeeze in his last fight, he cannot grapple at all. I’ve already spoken way too much about this slop but the last thing I want to say is at least McVey is making the most of his career; he left his bum gym in Missouri to train at the MMA Lab. He may be bad but he is at least getting real training. Dumas is training with whoever will take him and I doubt he is putting in the work seriously. McVey is really bad but I think he will beat this level of comp.

Bueno Silva vs Montague
Montague DEC

MBS getting another fight is crazy. She’s on a terrible skid and has looked extremely checked out; she barely makes any competitive effort to win and once you get her down she is going to sit in guard the entire fight and won’t make any attempts to get up. She is doing OF shoots with her GF now and I think is taking in one last check. This is a bit of a weird one though as I don’t think Montague is all that good. She’s extremely physical and a very good grappler, but she is a horrific striker and also 33, so we are likely not going to see any improvements out of her. Age doesn’t mean that much in WMMA but being so inexperienced at her age, I’m not really expecting big leaps fight to fight. This is a pretty similar dynamic to her debut except the opponent is obviously better; Montague needs to get tds to win and MBS needs to keep it standing. MBS is a grappler by trade and actually a pretty good black belt, but it’s clear at this point that she can’t stall in guard against real people. The ranked girls are too good to fall for the meme armbar from guard and she doesn’t do enough damage/activity to justify being on her back. MBS still has some skills, like she was landing good shots on Jasmine and even won a round against Chiasson, but she just completely checks out once you get her on her back. Her striking is better than Montague’s by a good amount (Montague legit can’t strike at all), but MBS’s process is nonexistent. She is a low iq brawler who takes one to give one and doesn’t make any adjustments whatsoever. She is the better striker here but idk how much it will matter since I expect Montague to get free takedowns. Montague’s technique and defense is alarmingly bad so if MBS shows some aggression I can see her having a lot of success here. Montague gets touched up almost every fight and was getting wobbled all over the place against Carolina, so MBS does have a clear POV. That being said, it’s so difficult to back her here since one takedown is likely the fight. I don’t even think Montague is that good of a wrestler/grappler, but she’s roided up, a former FW, and on that ATT supplement plan. These girls both train at ATT but when looking through socials I have no clue what MBS is doing currently. She is posting really everything but training and isn’t active to begin with. Montague is going hard at the gym with elite grapplers like Bea and Kayla, so she is getting good rounds in. Again, I don’t think she’s some super advanced grappler but she’s solid and clearly very strong. All she has to do is get MBS off balance and she will fall over. The round is over once Montague gets on top. MBS is content to play guard on her back and even if she wants to get up, I expect that to be really difficult since Montague is much stronger than her and has suppressive top control. For all her flaws, she is a crazy athlete and strong as ****; she is nonstop posting S&C regimens on her IG. I think the Montague fade is coming soon but not here; MBS is completely checked out. She is an OF model now (don’t ask how I know) and isn’t even posting MMA on her socials anymore. I think she is collecting one last paycheck here before she gets cut.

Filho vs Durden
Filho SUB RD 1

This fight got added quite literally as I was in the middle of doing breakdowns so I didn’t really tape it. That being said, I don’t think much research is needed here because this is an obvious finish for Filho. I can’t say I’m fully surprised that Durden is getting another fight (especially considering the names outside the UFC they offered Filho lol) but it’s wild seeing him back only 1.5 months removed from that disgusting showing against Tumendeberel. Cody looked shot to pieces in that fight and had a very confusing gameplan against someone who I think is pretty low level. Tumendeberel does have some good hardware for the division but on a technical level I think he’s really limited, yet he beat Durden at his own game. Durden kept panic wrestling and gassed himself out after 3 minutes shooting like an idiot, then he got taken down and reversed the rest of the fight. If this was on a full camp I would still favor Filho by a wide margin. On short notice, I don’t see what Durden can do to win this fight outside of a massive fluke. His cardio is bad, his wrestling looked massively regressed last fight (also very overrated in general), his striking is bad, his chin is gone, and he has terrible fight iq when it comes to submissions. I guess I could maybe see Durden having some success if he comes out how he did against Nascimento (Filho hates pressure and is hittable), but even then Filho is a serviceable striker with very underrated power. He stunned Carpenter pretty good before he got the takedown and of course got that sick KO back on DWCS. It’s just really hard to trust Durden to not run into something on the feet, especially considering how washed he’s looked lately. I want to say Durden will strike here since Filho is a grappler, but he’s never had good fight IQ and had no time to gameplan. I think Filho can get him down anyways but considering how good he is from his back, Durden may just shoot on him and get insta subbed. I don’t necessarily like Filho’s habits of playing from his guard but in this matchup I don’t think it matters much; the grappling Durden showed last fight was simply just atrocious. He was also getting taken down with little resistance so I think Filho should be fine in getting him down. He does have some of the classic BJJ nerd habits but his takedowns are actually pretty good & he’s very efficient when he does shoot. Once this hits the mat, regardless of who initiates it, I expect Filho to promptly find a sub. Durden is a complete idiot on the mat and has faced something like 20 submission attempts in the UFC. Filho’s bjj is some of the best in the division imo, and he can win from bottom or top. If Durden takes him down he can either sub him or find a reversal pretty easily. I expect him to get a takedown and get the back though. His top control is incredibly dominant and he is especially nasty from the body triangle. I picked Nasicmento over Filho but imo Filho looked like the better fighter there, and looking at Durden’s recent record I would argue Filho has the best bjj out of any of them (although its debatable with Nascimento). I think this is going to be a finish inside of 3 minutes here; Durden is jobbing for one last paycheck.

Griffin vs Valenzuela
Valenzuela DEC

Such a random, last minute addition but I honestly love this fight; I’m a big fan of these random signings. Even if these guys are not good, the fights are almost always entertaining. This is a bit of a weird one to break down; not sure how to feel about this line. Griffin does seem done and much like Moises last event, he’s one of those guys who is overrated because of who he’s fought. At the same time, this is a big jump in comp for Valenzuela, and he is going to fight the exact type of fight that Griffin thrives in. Valenzuela also gets hit a lot, kind of has a suspect chin, and is on the older side. I was tempted to back Griffin as he has wrestling he can go to, tons of experience, and historically is very durable but after tape idk; it’s hard because he doesn’t have a ton of bad losses but Griffin has looked pretty awful as of late. He is 41 at the end of this year and appears to be slowing down massively; his cardio was always bad but he’s clearly r1 or bust now, and doesn’t really do anything outside of r1. Griffin has been a front runner his entire career but these last 5-6 years he’s really looked terrible imo. He will start off hot and have an amazing r1, only to then stop fighting and check out. He has bad cardio but it’s also a mental thing as well; Griffin will just check out and stop trying to win even if he has the upper hand. A perfect example of this is his last two, both very winnable fights yet after r1 Griffin relegated himself to sitting on the outside doing nothing and allowing his opponents to come back into the fight. Griffin is 2-4 in his last 6 and his 2 wins were razor close splits where he barely did anything; in classic fashion, he won r1 fairly easily and then just stopped doing anything to win and made the fights very competitive. I still think he lost the Wells fight and the Means fight he would’ve clearly lost had it not been for a split second knockdown at the end of r2. This was the same back in his prime as well, as Griffin has a few bad split losses and many razor thin decision wins. These guys are both going to brawl with one another and both are hittable/sloppy so this is likely gonna come down to who can find the chin first. I want to favor Griffin as the experienced vet but I really don’t like backing mental midgets with bad cardio; especially against an aggressive brawler who will force a high pace the entire time. R1 I expect him to hurt Valenzuela big since he has bad defense and takes one to give one, but if he can’t close it out this is going to get really ugly really quickly. Griffin is just a terrible minute winner and it’s so hard to rely on him outside of r1. He gasses and checks out, and it’s really painful to back because he just refuses to do anything. I want to say he can win in r1 since Valenzuela is very hittable and is going to get right in his face, but if he survives the knockdown this will get scary; his chin might be a little sus but he has great recovery and is super tough. Valenzuela’s striking is nothing special but he can keep a pace and is not afraid to get into a war; I think Griffin is probably a better striker but I really hate his output and process. Someone who is going to step on the gas the entire fight is scary to fade against Griffin, as he needs a perfect 15 minutes and time to separate. Griffin may go to his wrestling here and I think he can score takedowns, but I don’t see him being able to wrestle for all 15 minutes. He doesn’t have the cardio for that and the last time we saw him take this gameplan (Imdaev) he looked like **** on the feet and got saved from a draw. If Valenzuela can survive the early onslaught, I think he is going to keep his foot on the gas and make Griffin really uncomfortable. Griffin has looked like **** the last few years and I don’t see him being able to sc**** by a greasy decision against someone with good cardio and aggression. Griffin is super durable but I have a feeling he will slow down big here and get finished late.

Alencar vs Polastri Polastri DEC
I don’t understand the pick rate here at all; this is such a wiki-capped/people randomly picking the girl with a better record scenario. To Alencar’s credit she has improved and done much more than I was ever expecting, but she’s still not good imo. Her last two fights have been complete layups against gassers with no tdd at all and similar frames to Alencar. Alencar is a very good grappler but her overall MMA game is really mid. Again, she has improved since joining the UFC but she’s still a very mediocre fighter with the same flaws she showed on dwcs. Her grappling is great but I still don’t think her takedowns have improved all that much. Her last two opponents don’t make any efforts to defend tds and once on their backs, do not get up and are basically useless. I can’t take anything from the Vanessa fight and Alencar did not look great against Carnelossi despite getting the win. She was still slowing down, struggling when put on the back foot, and had bad body language when made the nail. On paper Alencar does have a clear path to victory with her grappling, but imo its going to be an up hill battle; I think this matchup is very similar to the Luciano one on DWCS. Polastri has terrible grappling and fight iq but she is a menace on the feet; if Alencar can’t get tds here this is going to be a nightmare style for her. Polastri is far from perfect since she takes one to give one and loves to have stupid reactions on the feet, but Alencar can’t hang with her on the feet. Her striking has improved but she’s still very uncomfortable and gets hit a ton; she has t-rex arms as well, so she really struggles when girls make her the nail. Polastri will get hit but she will tank everything Alencar throws and there should be a significant difference in strikes landed imo. Alencar’s body language may hurt her a lot as well, since she panics when she’s the nail and really dislikes getting hit. Polastri has great conditioning and all she does on the feet is fight mean; she is going to pressure abd get in Alencar’s face swinging heat the entire time. Alencar will not be able to keep up nor does she have the power to get Polastri’s respect. The takedowns are a big concern here of course, since Polastri’s achilles heel is that she has no bottom game. Her first layer tdd is alright and she definitely knows how to grapple, but once on her back Polastri has no fight iq whatsoever. She is more than content to sit on her back the entire time and has had multiple perfomnances that were marred by her taking a round off; McKenna fight is a perfect example. Polastri legit bullied her for 12 minutes, McKenna couldn’t get tds at all, was clearly behind on the feet, and getting bullied in the clinch but after a perfect first two rounds, Polastri got taken down and just sat in guard the entire round. She has very bad fight iq and is lacking hard on the bottom. This worries me a lot since for all her flaws, Alencar has really good control. If she gets a takedown Polastri is going to be dumb enough to sit there and play bjj with her. However, I’m hoping the constant fortward pressure of Polastri will stop the tds from coming; it’s harder to shoot going backwards and pressure makes it so the tds become more predictable. Both of these girls are bad nails but Alencar really hates pressure and going backwards. If this stays standing I don’t think this is close at all; Alencar has struggled with this brawler archetype many times, and she definitely doesn’t have the cardio to withstand the pace of Polastri. I am kind of worried her iq fails her, but imo this is a really winnable fight. Alencar is old and the size of an atomweight, as well as being a one dimensional grappler. All Polastri needs to do here is pressure and not fight like a complete tard and I think she should win this easily. I’m not sold on Alencar and beating 2 girls with no tdd whatsoever does not tell me she’s ufc level.

[Predictions]

Jackson vs Barcelos
Barcelos DEC

Barcelos finally gets his shot at the top 15. Long overdue and it’s very impressive how he’s managed to look a career best after it seemed like he was done back in 2021-23. I never like backing a dog who is a tap fav by a sizable amount, but stylistically I think this is a favorable matchup for Barcelos. I liked Montel a lot a few years ago but the shine wore off for me when taping him for the Marcos fight. On paper he looks like a complete monster but his physical attributes carry him a lot; his actual skillset is nothing crazy. He does have insane power and has multiple one punch KO’s but to me he’s in a similar boat as Josh Emmett; he has some of the best power in the division but his KO threat is pretty overrated because he is a limited striker and also fought a lot of chinny guys/guys with no defense. He can definitely find the chin of Barcelos here but personally idk if the possibility of that happening is worth making him a favorite. Jackson is really dangerous but honestly he is not a great minute winner at all. In fact, when he isn’t on top or getting a knockdown, he doesn’t really do anything on his feet. He has a ridiculous reach of 75 inches and a good jab/straight (where most of his knockdowns come from) but he doesn’t really ever throw in combination nor more than one shot at a time. He is insanely low volume and is perfectly content to spar with you at range. It is always a worry that he can find the chin, especially against an older guy like Barcelos but so far only Umar has slept him. He can be dropped but his recovery is still very good. I didn’t like how he got clipped early against Simon, but Simon was also pressuring him against the cage swinging for the fences, which Jackson doesn’t do and has never done before. The reach and power will make it so that Barcelos has to be cautious, but he still forces a very high pace on the feet and imo is a much better boxer than Montel. Barcelos’s boxing fundamentals are great and he has really fast handf speed. He throws frequently, always gets in your face (key here as it takes away the range), and he is very good in the pocket. Simon is really washed but I was impressed how Barcelos was slipping the majority of his strikes and landing back. I think this activity and hand speed could really cause issues to Montek, as he needs a low pace and fighting at distance. Barcelos forces a hellacious pace in all of his fights too, so Montel will need to KO/hurt him or else I think he is going to have trouble getting the fight he wants. His physical qualities are insane but he’s just simply not a good minute winner, nor has he fought many good strikers either. Every time Montel has fought a quality striker (Figgy, Marcos, Arce) the fights were complete stinkers where neither guy threw anything. Montel was able to win the latter two because he did slightly more but he could’ve easily lost if they weren’t so petrified of being hit. I am worried Barcelos is cautious but he’s also such a vet that he should be fine. Durability aside I think he is a better striker anyways. One of Montel’s best attributes is his wrestling, but I think that too is overrated. He’s only dominated really low level guys on the mat and much like when he’s fought good strikers, guys who have good tdd have been able to neutralize it. Jackson is able to score takedowns but doing stuff with them proves difficult. His two losses prior to Figgy came by being outwrestled, and his tdd is shockingly bad considering he’s supposed to be this elite wrestler. Anyone who tries hard enough can take him down, and hes been outwrestled in all of his losses. Raoni shoots a ton but his offensive tds/control are actually not that good. However, he uses it to close the distance and disrupt his opponents rhythm. I think this will be effective here since again, it will keep the pace high and make Montel uncomfortable. It will also take away his reach advantage and make him even more passive than usual. I don’t see Barcelos holding him down for long but I can definitely see him getting takedowns and throwing off Montel’s game. However, I do not see Montel being able to do the same thing. Barcelos has always had very good tdd and is a Brazilian national wrestling champ/ 3rd degree black belt. He is more than prepared for the takedowns and nobody has had success outwrestling him over 15 minutes. Overall this is a close fight but I think Montel is being a little overrated on the betting line; imo he is not the scary guy he looks on paper. Against quality opposition he becomes very passive and his wrestling is not as elite as it seems. Barcelos is up there in age but he looks in career best shape (def on the juice) and is fighting some of the best fights of his career. Montel just lost his momentum and I think inactivity started to catch up to him last year. He is 34 and not the most dedicated fighter, so I think he will regress from fight to fight. He’s still a freak athlete but I don’t think this is as dangerous for Barcelos as fighting a 20-something. Again, I don’t like backing a dog that the public is all over but personally I think this is a good matchup for Raoni. Montel seems like he needs a finish here or else this is going to be a super competitive fight, and Raoni is the more well rounded fighter and better minute winner in that scenario. He also has the skillset to counter the wrestling and outwork Montel.

Buchecha vs Spann
Buchecha SUB RD 2

This fight has all the makings of a HW classic here: one dimensional bjj nerd vs glass cannon with nonexistent fight iq, both on short notice. I feel bad for Buchecha here, he finally got an easy fight in Gimenis and now has to fight yet another experienced HW (funny enough, the highest ranked guy out of all his opponents) and the most dangerous one at that. In some ways this is one of Buchecha’s hardest fights so far but in others, also the easiest. Spann is one of the dumbest fighters on the roster and doesn’t train that seriously either. He has a good skillset but is carried by the fact that he’s a freak athlete; if he took the sport seriously I actually think he could’ve been a very good fighter. It’s impossible to know what Spann is up to anyways, as he is silent on social media and never posting training photos. I know he’s no longer at Fortis full time and Sayif Saud was the only guy pushing him to be serious, and the results have shown. Buchecha has had a pretty bad run to start in the UFC but I think this is one of his easier fights in terms of being able to get the fight he wants. Unfortunately, he’s fighting another massive human being but his prior two opponents had very good tdd and defensive wrestling/grappling, neither of which are present in Spann’s game. A gust of wind could take Spann down and he doesn’t offer much of anything if he doesn’t get his signature guillotine. Buchecha has been a flop so far but him getting caught in that would be incredibly shocking. As long as he avoids that, Buchecha will be able to score easy takedowns here, and even dominate on top. He is a bjj player at heart, so he will do dumb **** like pull guard or go for leg attacks, but even then Spann is bad/dumb enough that he could end up in a heel hook or get swept. It was almost a decade ago but the Drysale fight is extremely relevant to me here, as he is pretty much an identical matchup to Buchecha. He was scoring relatively easy takedowns on Spann in r1 but Spann’s athleticism allowed him to explode to his feet and get to positions quicker. He did hurt Drysdale bad at times but was never able to close it (an issue he frequently has), and fumbled the win badly. He gassed out after 4 minutes and gave up some piss poor takedowns. To start r2, Drysdale looked done yet immediately got a takedown, took the back with ease, and then dominated Spann for a finish. After watching his UFC run again, I honestly see this fight playing out very similarly. On the feet Spann should be able to smoke Buchecha, but his problem is that he has no process whatsoever. He hits very hard but punches from his waist and doesn’t know what footwork is at all. As a result, he has hurt many guys yet only has 6 wins by KO. Seeing him fumble the Drysdale matchup was really bad to me because we’ve seen similar grit from Buchecha. He had a bad debut but in the Kennedy fight he showed the same resolve that I liked in the Reug Reug fight; after a bad ending to r1 and a brutal eye poke, he got a huge blast double in r2 and then dominated the round to get a draw. I think Spann is live to absolutely nuke him on the feet, but if he doesn’t get the finish initially he will let Buchecha back into the fight. He’s done this in the ufc as well, fumbling the Smith rematch was one of the lowest iq moments I’ve ever seen; he had Smith completely done in r2 but instead of going for it he decided to strike with smith in r3 and just sparred with him, letting him steal the round and the fight. I have to favor Spann on the feet here but I think his awful footwork and overswinging will give Buchecha openings to get takedowns, and his iq is so bad that I think Bucecha can survive bad moments like he has in the past. If this hits the mat, Buchecha will dominate him. He has yet to get a finish in the ufc but he has dominated when he’s gotten on top. With more cardio and better decision making, he could’ve finished Kennedy in r2 (he also had the argument for a 10-8 anyways). Spann is a bad grappler with bad iq, and he has been finished many times. I think this is an easier fight for him in terms of being able to dominate on the mat since Spann is extremely low iq and doesn’t defend takedowns. I do worry about him being able to explode to his feet but as this fight gets extended he is going to slow down tremendously. Buchecha gasses too but he at least doesn’t quit on himself and will try to win, Spann checks out and everything goes out the window once he gasses. Spann is also on short notice so I expwect his already awful fight iq/cardio to be even worse. It feels bad backing Buchecha since his style has proven to be archaic in the UFC, but I really think Spann is a good fight for him to find a finish in.

[Predictions]

Sterling vs Zalal
Zalal DEC

I really dislike both of these guys and their styles but I’m looking forward to this one. There’s a good chance it ends up being really boring and is another classic split decision where neither guy did much, but I'm hoping these guys actually show up to fight. Zalal has improved a lot since his first run but he’s also got one of the worst winstreaks in the division. Everyone he’s fought is either completely washed, cut, or both. Only one out of the five guys he’s beaten has actually won a fight since Zalal fought him (Jarno) and it was on the regional scene. He’s been getting a lot of favorable matchups against guys on their way out so it’s very difficult to gauge how good he actually is. Zalal always had a well rounded skillset but his problem was that he just wouldn’t do enough in his fights. He lost a lot of fights the first time around because he wouldn’t throw and would fall behind. He still fights the same way imo, he’s just found his groove on the feet and gotten better with his grappling. I want to say he can be a contender but imo his run reminds me a lot of Buckley’s initial run at 170; sure he’s winning a lot and picking up finishes but a lot of his old flaws are still there, and he’s fighting really bad guys. On the feet I think Zalal should cruise here but my main worry is the grappling. Stirling is such an annoying weasel with his grappling. He is so good at chain wrestling and once he gets ahold of you he does not let go. He doesn’t do much damage and he can be reversed/stuffed/taken down yet he just finds a way to keep hustling and end up on top. His wrestling somehow got better at 145, at 135 his accuracy was awful and he’d need to shoot like 5-6 times just for one td but now he’s ragdolling guys who are bigger than him. Aljo has put on a ton of muscle since moving to 145 and has gotten much stronger as a result. Zalal is a good grappler but this will be a true litmus test of how much he’s improved. He’s had a huge grappling advantage in every fight since coming back (besides the shore fight) and hasn’t had his defensive wrestling tested in a while. He did get taken down a couple times on this current run but nothing came of it. My biggest issue is the Blackshear fight. Now this is obviously a weird one since it was at 135 but imo he’s the closest comp to Aljo that Zalal has fought, and Zalal fought like a complete tard. I assume his mindset was different since he was fighting for his job but he spent the first two rounds going 50/50 in grappling exchanges and kept allowing Blackshear to get the upperhand, before cooking him in r3 and avoiding the loss. Zalal was defending the takedowns well but he kept making stupid ass mistakes to get reversed and when he did get on top, he did nothing with it.  On a quick side tangent, both of these guys have an AWFUL habit of doing the bare minimum to win fights; they have so many greasy decisions on their records because they refuse to close fights strong. Back to the Blackshear fight, this worries me because I think Aljo can replicate this gameplan fairly easily. Zalal is squirmy and does get to his feet but he can be taken down and has not shown that his wrestling, or even his grappling is on this level. I think Aljo is on a clear decline but he’s still an elite grappler so I’m worried he gets the backpack going. That being said, I think cardio is a huge edge for Zalal here. That sounds ironic considering he loves to do absolutely jack **** but Aljo’s cardio is not good. He would always slow down in the champ rounds back at 135 and that showed in his last fight. The Ortega fight as a whole was just a really bad look to me, Ortega died making weight and Aljo just ran away sparring with him the entire fight, and somehow gassed out in r4/5. He blamed it on being old but the fact he had a light sparring session against a heavy bag and still slowed down was really bad scenes to me. Not like Aljo is a gas bag or anything, but if he does slow down and try to cruise I think Zalal is going to be smart enough to step on the gas. He showed in the Blackshear/Quarantillo fights that he easily can force a pace when he wants to. Even if he is losing the grappling early (which I expect), If Zalal is forcing a lot of scrambles and making Aljo work to hold him down, that will pay dividends late. I am worried about him getting stuck there and losing the rounds like in the Topuria fight, but Aljo’s backpacking hasn’t been as effective here; he mostly just resorts to td spam. I hate how Zalal doesn’t throw anything but I think we will see him come out aggressive once he gets space to work. Aljo is a good striker but nothing special, and in current form he is harmless and wants nothing to do with an actual fight. He is going to run away and counter with his hands and leg kicks. Zalal does a lot of the same but he has sneaky knees that he will mix in and much more confidence as a striker. I think he has the ability to pour it on late and I expect his pace to be a big advantage in the champ rounds here. I think this is probably going to come down to a close round 3 but I favor Zalal here as teh younger guy with more momentum. I don’t think Aljo can push himself the same way in 5 rounders anymore. This is a weird one though because I’m not sold on Zalal as a contender whatsoever. If he can put Aljo away I’ll buy some stock but other than that I don’t see him going much further than this. I have decision because these guys love to do nothing, but I would not be shocked by a late KO in the champ rounds from Zalal.

Dumont vs Edwards
Dumont DEC

Really weird fight. Edwards is being rewarded for her finishes by drawing the top contender in the weight class. Definitely deserved but I’m not sure I can get behind her here. Dumont is always in a weird spot because she’s been incredibly consistent yet never gets big opportunities because she’s old, doesn’t finish fights, and always fights really close. Her last fight was another case of this, being a really boring, really close split decision that she got lucky to win. I feel like UFC is trying to get rid of her and shake up the dead BW division, but I’m not really sure this is her time yet. Edwards has improved a lot but she’s still extremely flawed. I remember during the pandemic when she was a complete meme fighter; missing weight every fight, barely eeking out close decisions, and white belt level grappling. Her grappling has gotten a lot better and she’s shown a lot more aggression lately, but she’s still the same fighter imo. I say this because I confidently backed her to beat Cornolle in the rematch, yet she looked awful until Cornolle got injured on the slam. She was losing the striking, showing bad body language, and wasn’t wrestling despite being a really obvious path to victory. I think this is just going to be a really annoying levels fight. Edwards has some power but her boxing is really sloppy. She always leaves her chin out in the air and has struggled hard against better boxers. Dumont is a boring point fighter but she doesn’t make a ton of mistakes and is very technical. I think she should be able ton take advantage of the openings Edwards leaves. Edwards may try to grapple but I don’t see her having a ton of success there either. Her offensive tds are simply not that good and Norma is difficult to take down due to her size. I wouldn’t be shocked if this is a close fight and they rob Dumont since the UFC wants her gone, but I don’t see Edwards going from beating up unranked cans to beating a perennial top contender, as beatable as she may be.

Garcia vs Hernandez
Hernandez KO RD 2

I was a little shocked to see the odds here, feels like the books can never properly gauge a Hernandez fight. Rafa got a statement win last time out but on rewatch he didn’t look super impressive. Gordon also ****** his knee up overnight in a complete freak accident, and it showed in that fight. Can’t take away from Garcia but Gordon was so flat footed because his leg was ****** up. Garcia is also really inconsistent; sometimes he looks great and other times he looks like he did when he first came to the ufc; bad cardio, sloppy striking, very hittable, etc. Like I said, on rewatch the Gordon fight was not super impressive. Besides being compromised, Gordon was landing a lot on Garcia and froze him in place multiple times in r2. The thing about Garcia is that he’s solid everywhere but doesn’t excel anywhere. He’s unathletic, not powerful, and not super threatening either. Hernandez has always been a really skilled guy, his problems have all been on the mental side of things. He also got rushed too quickly and struggled to find his footing as well. What really ****** him up was the move to 145, without that he’d be on a 5 fight winstreak and his last loss being over 4 years ago. I think this is a really good matchup for him, Hernandez will have a huge advantage here in terms of speed, footwork, and athleticism. Garcia doesn’t move that well and is pretty slow; part of the reason he did so well against Gordon was because he just stood in the pocket not moving and let Garcia tee off of him. Hernandez is always moving and forcing different looks, a big part of his game recently has been getting his opponents to chase and countering back. A caveat of this style is that he can be thin margins, but whenever he lands it's always a big shot. I feel like he should be able to outwork Garcia pretty easily here. Garcia is a solid striker but nothing special to me. He doesn’t have enough power or pressure well enough to cause a ton of issues, and he can’t rely on takedowns to win. He needs a scrap up close and Hernandez is just too good at evading that and getting the fight he wants. I was originally thinking this is an easy decision but after seeing the Gordon fight im not so sure. Rafa is tough and durable but he was getting rocked all over the place in that last fight. I don’t think he can eat clean shots like that here, and I thought his defense looked really sloppy the entire time. Rafa is also at a big athleticism disadvantage here, which will really hurt him because he’s just an average guy. I don’t think he has his usual cardio edge here either since Hernandez has seemingly fixed that. He only gasses when made teh nail and Garcia isn’t really a great pressure fighter. His cardio is also very inconsistent as well; sometimes he can go 10 rounds and other times he’s exhausted at the halfway mark. Overall I expect Hernandez to win this easily. I think this is a step down from CDF and feel like Hernandez should be fighting someone ranked. Garcia is just a mid level gatekeeper to me.

Grant vs Martinetti
Grant KO RD 3

I always love a Grant fight and they are giving him a good dancing partner here in Martinetti. I don’t really get the matchmaking here though, UFC did the same thing last event with Vologdin’s debut as well; matching both of these guys against experienced vets who can fight the exact same style as them. I understand it from a matchmaking perspective but for a debut these two fights don’t make a lot of sense. Martinetti is a good fighter but I don’t know if he’s ready for this jump in competition. I was willing to make the assumption for Vologdin because of the styles, but Martinetti is getting a much tougher opponent. I like Vologdin but I’m also not sure he belongs in the UFC; he’s very talented but he’s way too small to be at 135 and is a punching bag. That fight was an all timer but Martinetti was getting tuned up in the pocket early. He won because he was able to impose his will on the small Vologdin, but I was not super impressed with him while Vologdin was fresh. The resolve and the pace he fought at was impressive, especially since he’d never shown that in prior fights, but I also think a big part of that was because he was able to eat Vologdin’s shots and bully him since he was so small; that will not be an option against Grant. Grant is 40 with a lot of mileage which is always dangerous but I think he’s still in relatively good shape. He’s been competing at a decently high level this entire time so while he is at the end of his career, I don’t think he’s necessarily “washed”. It’s also difficult to gauge how good Martinetti actually is, it remains to be seen if Vologdin can last in the ufc and prior to that he was padding his record against cans in Mexico. I do think he has a well rounded skillset and has good conditioning but he’s also super hittable, has no power, and I’m not sure his grappling will be effective against high level competition. In his fights prior to dwcs Martinetti was scrappy, but never fighting at that insane pace. He was actually pretty measured and likes to fight behind his reach, or go to his grappling. Seeing how Vologdin looked against Castaneda, I’m starting to think it was more his style that caused that fight rather than Martinetti being some wild brawler. He can definitely recreate that against Grant but it’s going to be very difficult. Besides all the experience and craftiness, Grant is a very unique striker and is the exact same size as Martinetti. He isn’t very conventional but he rips these really nasty hooks from all sorts of weird angle, as well as throwing a lot of kicks. He has a lot of power and is surprisingly fast for how old he looks. At 40 it’s possible his durability is going, and he does slow down in crazy fights but for the most part I think he can hold up. Martinetti doesn’t have the power to hurt him and I don’t think he can brawl in the pocket like last fight without getting ktfo. Sure, he’s tough but he doesn’t move his head at all and was eating everything Vologdin threw at him. He was able to eat that because Vologdin doesn’t have power, Grant does. If he tries to walk into the pocket with his tall man’s defense he is going to get sat on his ass. Even with the size gap, I think if Vologdin had any power at 135 he could’ve won that fight; Martinetti was just eating everything unphased and was able to walk him down as a result. Grant does struggle with guys who can outbrawl him. Martinetti does have a pace edge but I think it will be difficult to get that going because he’s hittable and lacks power. I could see Grant punching himself out early and Martinetti coming back, but he would have to go through hell first. I don’t think Martinetti will have a ton of success with his grappling either. He is a solid black belt but his wrestling is meh, and also untested. Grant can be taken down and has been held in the body triangle before, but for the most part he is hard to take and hold down. Martinetti can get him down but I don’t think his control is that great. In most of his regional fights when he doesn’t get an early sub he usually just rides out top control. In some of his fights he gets really sloppy and gets reversed or falls off the back. Grant has been subbed a bunch but he’s still a really crafty grappler and you have to beat him up to get the sub, he won’t tap while fresh. This could be a case of too many wars and Grant slows down, but even in current form I think he’s much better than Martinetti. His dwcs fight was an instant classic but that doesn’t mean he’s instantly a top 25 level BW. He has some good tools but I think this is a really tough ask for your ufc debut.

Finally a card with lots of familiar faces. I actually got a few strong gut responses to some of these matchups, but I'll have to look into them all a bit before I post any serious comments or anything.

I mean, it could be anything - including a repeat of Kattar round 3 but for more rounds. Zalal would absolutely just run around and out point Aljo for a win if he wanted to, that's why I like him. He's going to do what he has to to win, that's that. CRIMINAL he didn't get a performance bonus against Emmet. Absolutely criminal.

I do think that at a glance the Mconico fight is strangely weighted. I'm sorry but a loss to Nickal isn't a positive mark on your record like some people might think, just like a win against Brundage isn't a disgrace to have. posts.content:
[Predictions]

I really don't like Aljo as a top level fighter in 2026. Don't like the way many people try to undermine his achievements because of the way he won the belt, but he defended it 3 times, and now looks more technical than ever at 145. However, the reason I said I don't like him is because I just don't think he's that good anymore. His style worked well against Movsar, but he was afraid of getting the fight to the ground against the corpse of Brian Ortega. He mostly striked from distance but with very low volume, so it's clear his cardio isn't great anymore, and he looks like he doesn't want to engage. He didn't look great in rounds 4 and 5, and all of his 5 round fights that went past the 3rd didn't look great aswell. He's clearly on a physical decline, and to know exactly how big that decline is, we need to see how he looks across the distance this weekend, if neither he or Zalal finish the fight earlier. I still rate his peak much higher than where Zalal is at right now, but to me Aljo is in a similar situation as Brandon Moreno where people still think it's 2022 and he's in his athletic prime.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

I think Garcia backers should rewatch the Gordon fight, it was not as flawless as it looks. For starters, that fight never should’ve taken place. Gordon confirmed he quite literally got run over by a car the day before in a freak accident, and you could clearly see he had one knee in that fight. Gordon just stood in the pocket all fight and had no movement whatsoever. Even then he still made it competitive; I had an image in my head that Rafa slowly styled on him until he gassed out and got ko’ed, but Gordon was actually teeing off of him. Raf honestly did a terrible job of fighting to Gordon’s weaknesses; instead of utilizing footwork to style on the guy who could only move vertically, he just stood in the pocket and banged with Gordon. Gordon rocked him multiple times and froze him in place with some big hooks about three times in r2. Gordon also has no chin and couldn’t avoid strikes, so while impressive I can’t take a ton from Rafa sleeping him; he defintely is not doing that to Hernandez and has no power either. I think Hernandez is goin to dance around him in the feet and the speed/athleticism gap is going to be crazy here. People forget since he went to 145 but Hernandez hasn’t lost at 155 in over 4 years; Moicano was the last guy to beat him at this weight class in Feb 2022

This all comes down to if Davey can defend the takedowns or not. If he can keep it standing his boxing will cause big problems for Martinetti and he's going to bust him up. posts.author: Foxyy
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Aljo and Zalal are the main event this weekend. Aljo's got a lot of championship experience, and this is Zalal's first main event. This fight is at the apex; I guess Zalal is such a star they're actively afraid to put him in front of a crowd. Riveting stuff. Looking at this fight, Aljo has the clear wrestling, pressure, and experience advantage, while Zalal has a clear advantage in striking, movement, momentum, and general well-roundedness. From a gameplanning perspective, I think they're about even; both fighters are going to come in with proper gameplans.

To state the obvous, Aljo's win condition here is consistently taking Zalal down and controlling him while Zalal's win condition is outstriking Aljo on the feet. Aljo will need to close distance in order to take Zalal down, and Zalal will need to deploy a movement-heavy gameplan to make it harder for Aljo to close and shoot. To take away Zalal's movement, Aljo will pressure, use the small apex cage, and low kicks. To keep the fight on the feet, Zalal will be continuously sticking and moving, and feinting with knees.

I expect the fight to start out rather slow, with both fighters feeling things out. Zalal will likely win minutes early, and once Aljo shoots, the fight begins. I expect the most pivotal work to be done in rounds 2 and 3. I think Aljo will have some difficulty getting Zalal down and controlling him, and when he gets there, he won't be able to dominate Zalal in my opinion. I also think it will be somewhat difficult for Zalal to effectively manage distance during this time. As the fight progresses, I expect Zalal to build. Aljo is older now and his cardio doesn't fare well here theoretically. With the work he'll have to put in to get Zalal down, he will tire out. He will have to use his massive experience edge here. I expect the first half of the fight to be quite competitive, with the second half being in favor of Zalal.

Conclusion:
Aljo will need to rely on winning 1-3. Zalal will need to do good work in the first half of the fight and take advantage of his late edge. I see Zalal winning rounds 4-5 at a high clip here. The most likely outcome here in my opinion is Zalal winning 3-2 or 4-1. Aljo has an outside shot, but to be honest I think everything has to go right for him to win.

Prediction: Zalal by decision.

I think this is a pivotal fight for both fighters. Zalal will now he in the near-title picture, likely 1 win away. Aljo will be over 35 years old and outside of the top 5. It will be difficult for him to find the confidence to push for the title further if he were to lose here.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

@flavorpacket i think he should be able to yeah. If he wants to brawl with Grant I think Martinetti will get cooked with his lack of defense/power. Grants cardio generally is pretty good but at 40 it defintely is a worry. The obly guy to actually break him down in a brawl was Chito back in 2022, and Martinetti obviously is not at that level. He did slow a bit against Smolka as well, but then ko’ed him in r3 so maybe he was holding for a second wind; he got a 10-8 in r1 and absolutely destoryed him early. Martinetti does seem decently tough so I am worried that he could take a beating early and swamp Grant, but that seems unlikely.

Even if Martinetti grapples I don’t think he will have a ton of success. Grant can be taken down but he’s a pretty solid black belt and is hard to hold there. Martinetti’s grappling seems average at best on tape so I think Grant should be fine. Martinetti is a black belt too but he’s got mid positional control and pretty bad wrestling too. The only way you sub Grant is if you beat him up like Jourdain or gas him out.

I honestly think Grant has him covered everywhere, it just comes down to age. Even then while there is a decade age gap, Martinetti is 30 so it’s not like he’s facing some youthful, top prospect. He’s fighting a finished product with a flashy record. Grant is ancient but he still seems to be operating at a high level. He’s consistently been fighting high level comp and every time he’s been matched with someone at this level, he’s usually smoked them. I think Martinetti is obviously better than Tavares but this does seem like a similar scenario to me; Grant being close odds against a younger guy who was just in a slugfest, but isn’t actually that good of a fighter. Grant ended up schooling him in a 30-27 and doubling his output. Grant also moved to Vegas full time about a year or two ago, so he still seems pretty dialed in

I dont usually factor it in much but this is also a massive step up in comp for Martinetti. His record was padded with cans in Mexico and he went life and death with the first real guy he fought, so he honestly may just be a fraud. I like vologsin a lot and think he’s more skilled than most, but he should defintely be at 125 and it remains to be seen if he even belongs at this level. Grant has fought such a higher level of competition and given dog fights to some very solid guys, I’d be pretty shocked if he lost this one. Would have to be due to age catching up to him after the last fight

posts.author: Foxyy
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Buchecha vs. Spann
Marcus Buchecha, SUB, R2
I think Spann wins r1 and then  Curtis Blaydes is the most dominant fundamental wrestler the division has ever seen, possessing a double-leg takedown so explosive and technically sound that it feels like an inevitability rather than a strike. Blaydes doesn't just compete; he imposes a grueling, high-pressure pace that suffocates opponents, blending world-class grappling with a vastly improved striking game. His ability to chain wrestling sequences together while maintaining the gas tank of a middleweight makes him a nightmare matchup for anyone in the world, proving time and again that he is the ultimate "gatekeeper to the gold" who consistently shuts down the hype of rising contenders. Beyond the raw physicality, there is a profound level of resilience and tactical intelligence in Blaydes’ game. He has navigated one of the toughest schedules in UFC history, never shying away from the most dangerous knockout artists in the sport. While others might play it safe, Blaydes trusts in his "Razor" elbows and his ability to neutralize chaos with calculated control. He is a true professional who stays out of the drama and lets his work inside the Octagon do the talking. Whether he’s setting records for the most takedowns in heavyweight history or showing off his evolved kickboxing, Blaydes remains a perennial title threat and a masterclass in how to weaponize elite wrestling in MMA.

posts.author: Belal Sucks
posts.content:
[Predictions]

García vs. Hernandez
Rafa García, DEC
I just got a gut feeling about this one here. Something strange was definitely going on to call off Hernandez’s last fight and I think at the very least that’s a distraction that he’s dealing with in preparation for this fight. The fight itself is a pretty tough matchup too considering the pressure and pace of Garcia to go against Hernandez’s patient and counter heavy “sniper” approach. I think honestly even though Hernandez has the better skills I have to go Rafa here soley for his minute winning ability in this matchup. Hernandez is either going to have to rely on a big knockout or resort to grappling if we wants to win this fight and I just don’t see either happening.

Buchecha vs. Spann
Ryan Spann, TKO, R1
Been kind of back and forth with this one. Ultimately I’m picking Spann because Buchecha hasn’t won a UFC fight yet and this is arguably his toughest test. Ryan Spann has every reason to win this fight but the problem is that he’s Ryan Spann. He’s about as inconsistent as they come so a matchup like this feels like there’s a wide possibility of outcomes. I’d be more worried if Buchecha had big power to go with his grappling but since he doesn’t I think Spann is going to be in the pocket swinging heavy.

Bueno Silva vs. Montague
Michelle Montague, DEC
Bueno Silva was never good and her somehow fluking her way to a title fight will always be funny to me. I’m gonna take Montague but on a side note I don’t think she’s all that either. She seems a bit green on the feet and she’s not exactly running through these girls with her grappling like Bia Mesquite. Still, the division needs some life so after she scoops up an easy win here I could see a big push coming.

Filho vs. Durden
Jafel Filho, SUB, R2
Durden…. What are we doing buddy? Either take a fight with a full camp or just hang it up. Signing yourself up for these matchups where you have zero chance is making me think he’s just collecting a couple checks on his way out.

Marshall vs. Brennan
Francis Marshall, SUB, R1
Another layup for Marshall I suppose.

Griffin vs. Valenzuela
Max Griffin, DEC
I’m going with the crafty vet here. He’s definitely not what he used to be which granted he was always pretty average but I like him here. Guys who are 0-1 on contender serries are typically a must fade and Valenzuela might just be a cool name merchant. If Griffin was a can I’d probably take the younger guy but hes always been kind of decent and that’s probably enough to get it done here.

Alencar vs. Polastri
Julia Polastri, DEC
Kind of an interesting matchup. Not super high on either woman but it’s a classic striker vs grappler matchup which always makes for a compelling fight. I think I like Plostri’s ability to do more damage and be the aggressor here. I could see a possible mat smushing by Alencar but from the small sample size I’ve seen from her she doesn’t seem to have a great gas tank.

[Predictions]

Max Griffin vs Victor Valenzuela

Griffin has been in the UFC forever, he’s quite a limited fighter as far as skill, but he does everything ok and doesn’t have any glaring weaknesses apart from death gassing a few times in fights. Moves around a lot on the feet, ok jab and will go for a takedown in nearly all of his fights at least once. Always been a middle to lower in the pack in the UFC.

Valenzuela himself isn’t talented or special either, decent stand up and has some power, at 5”9 he’s always going to be at a disadvantage at 170 with the reach.

I like Victor here, I think Griffin may have a little success early with his jab and Victor will struggle to land on him early, but as the fight goes on Victor should land more as Griffin’s movement reduces on the feet. Victor could get the finish as Griffin is 40 now, but I’ve always found Griffin to handle himself pretty well whenever he gets hurt, even when he’s gassed as **** he still manages to keep his defences up and just survive in there. Only way I see Griffin taking this is if Valenzuela doesn’t go after Griffin and he lets Griffin play his game by staying on the outside throwing single strikes and not coming forward and pressuring. Valenzuela via Decision

It’s honestly embarrassing that people are still wasting their weekends on that Paramount UFC slop when WrestleMania just proved it’s the objectively superior MMA organization. While Dana White’s roster is busy hugging each other on the floor for three rounds, WWE fighters are out here landing 450 splashes and devastating powerbombs that would literally knock a UFC heavyweight unconscious in seconds.

The lack of innovation in the Octagon is staggering—every "fighter" has the same buzzcut and uses the same recycled leg kicks, whereas WWE fighters are actually unique individuals who bring innovative, high-risk maneuvers to the ring that require way more technical skill than a standard jab.

It’s a total no-brainer that the strikers in the WWE have more raw power and actually finish fights instantly instead of waiting for a judge’s scorecard. If you actually care about elite combat sports, you’d realize that these fighters are physically dominant specimens compared to the interchangeable mid-tier roster the UFC keeps pushing.

Imagine thinking a "takedown" is more effective than a literal Tombstone Piledriver; the "Ultimate" league is just pure cope for people who can't handle the intensity of a truly innovative heavyweight clash.
